context:
Vibrations and waves are everywhere. If you take any system and disturb it from a stable equilibrium, the resultant motion will be waves and vibrations. Think of a guitar string—pluck the string, and it vibrates. The sound waves generated make their way to our ears, and we hear the string’s sound. Our eyes see what’s happening because they receive the electromagnetic waves of the light reflected from the guitar string, so that we can recognize the beautiful sinusoidal waves on the string. In fact, without vibrations and waves, we could not recognize the universe around us at all!

The amazing thing is that we can describe many fascinating phenomena arising from very different physical systems with mathematics. This course will provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. You will learn that waves come from many interconnected (coupled) objects when they are vibrating together. We will discuss many of these phenomena, along with related topics, including mechanical vibrations and waves, sound waves, electromagnetic waves, optics, and gravitational waves.

Syllabus:
8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves

Prerequisites:
18.02 Multivariable Calculus and 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism.

This course is designed for students who are already familiar with the concepts taught in 8.01 Classical Mechanics as well as 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism. We also rely on fundamental mathematics principles taught in 18.01 Single Variable Calculus and 18.02 Multivariable Calculus, as well as some aspects of 18.06 Linear Algebra.

Course Overview:
What are vibrations? Many physical systems vibrate or oscillate when something disrupts their equilibrium. Think of what happens when you pull a mass attached to a spring or push a pendulum in a certain direction. The mass bounces up and down. The pendulum swings back and forth. But even these simple phenomena can respond in complicated and counterintuitive ways when subjected to forces known in physics as damping and driving. Combining several pendulums or springs can lead to even more unexpected motions. In this course you will see how physics can describe these motions mathematically.

Extended physical systems that have been made to vibrate, like a string on a guitar, cannot return to their state of equilibrium without exerting forces on the area around them. These forces then lead to the phenomenon of waves, disturbances that propagate through a medium. The vibrating guitar string causes a sound wave to propagate through the medium of the air.

Among the most fascinating phenomena examined in the course are electromagnetic (EM) waves. EM waves have many properties similar to the waves we experience in everyday life, but the underlying physics of EM waves is profoundly different. Instead of a local disturbance exerting a force on adjoining regions, changing magnetic fields create electric fields (as quantified by Faraday's Law), and changing electric fields create magnetic fields (as quantified by Maxwell's extension of Ampere's Law).

Course Goals:
This course has two complementary goals. The first is to provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. This will allow you to gain a deeper appreciation for the true nature and beauty of phenomena like music and rainbows, which all of us observe or experience every day.

The second goal is to provide you with the skill of using a broad range of techniques that can greatly simplify the analysis and solution of complex systems. These techniques include complex numbers, combinations of oscillatory and exponentially decaying functions, resonance, normal modes, Cramer's rule for solving several equations in several variables, boundary conditions, general wave equations, Fourier decomposition, dispersive and non-dispersive media, phase and group velocities, sound cavities and wave guides, polarization, Doppler effect, reflection and refraction, Fresnel's equation for transmission and reflection, total internal reflection, constructive and destructive interference, and diffraction.

Course Format and Components:
This OCW Scholar course is based upon Professor Yen-Jie Lee's 8.03 Physics III: Vibrations and Waves course as taught on the MIT campus. It includes a complete set of 24 video lectures recorded at MIT in the Fall of 2016. Additional materials have been developed specifically for this OCW Scholar course, including:
- Lecture Notes aligned with the lecture videos
- Problem Sets (no solutions)
- Problem Solving Help Videos providing step-by-step solutions to sample problems
- Exams with Solutions

MIT students spend about 150-200 hours learning Vibrations and Waves in the on-campus version of this course. That number comes from a combination of attending lectures and recitations, and studying independently. It's difficult to estimate how long it will take you to complete all of the modules in this particular course, but you can probably expect to spend 3 to 4 hours on practice problems, readings and assessment for each hour of lecture video you watch.

Required Textbook:
Georgi, Howard. The Physics of Waves. Benjamin Cummings, 1992. ISBN: 9780136656210. Download the book (PDF - 5.5MB) (Courtesy of Howard Georgi. Used with permission.)

Suggested References:
French, A. P. Vibrations and Waves. New York: W.W. Norton, 1980. ISBN: 9780393099362.

Bekefi, George, and Alan H. Barrett. Electromagnetic Vibrations, Waves, and Radiation. MIT Press, 1977. ISBN: 9780262520478. [Preview with Google Books]

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 6

8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Fall 2016
Homework 6


Problem 6.1 (25 pts)

Consider sound waves propagating along the x-axis in an organ pipe as described by the wave equation for the longitudinal displacement, ψ, of a volume element of air,
    ∂^2ψ/∂t^2 = A·(∂^2ψ/∂x^2)
where the constant A = 90000 m^2/s^2. The organ pipe is closed at one end, x = 0, and open at the other end, x = L = 1.5 m.

(a) Make a drawing of the first 3 normal modes over the interval 0 ≤ x ≤ L (i.e., sketch ψ as a function of x at maximum displacement), and write down the normal mode frequencies for each. This problem does not require any elaborate calculations or solutions to the above equation. All you need is just a physical understanding of how the normal modes are determined and some very rudimentary arithmetic.
(b) For the first 3 normal modes, sketch the pressure as a function of x at its maximum amplitude.
(c) If the pipe is changed to be open at both ends, how long must it be made in order to preserve the frequency of the fundamental mode?


Problem 6.2 (25 pts)

During the lecture, we discussed the wave solution from Maxwell's equations. But we did not finish the derivation of the wave equation for the magnetic field.

(a) Show that ∇ × (∇ × A) = ∇(∇ · A) − (∇ · ∇)A, where A is a vector.
(b) Show that in vacuum, ∇^2·B = µ0·ε0·(∂^2B/∂t^2), using Maxwell's equations.


Problem 6.3 (25 pts)

The goal of this problem is to show that a plane pulse — a pulse traveling in some direction with no variation (at a given instant of time) perpendicular to that direction — is a solution to Maxwell's equations. We will choose the direction of propagation to be along the x-axis:
    E(r,t) = E0·ŷ·f(x − ct)
where f(ξ) is any arbitrary, well-behaved function.

(a) Show that this field satisfies the EM wave equation.
(b) Show that the field satisfies ∇ · E = 0. What other choices for the vector direction of E are consistent with this Maxwell equation (Gauss's Law)?
(c) Find an expression for the magnetic field associated with this pulse.


Problem 6.4 (25 pts)

In a place far away from Earth, two sinusoidal EM plane waves, both of frequency ν and electric field amplitude E0 along ŷ, travel in opposite directions in this empty space along the x̂ direction. At t = 0, the electric field is found to be 0 at x = 0.

(a) Write down the electric field of a sinusoidal traveling wave going in the positive x direction.
    Hint: See lecture note 12, page 6 and the example discussed during the lecture. In general, the electric field of a progressing sinusoidal EM wave can be written as E(r,t) = Re(E0·e^(j(k·r − ωt))), where r = x·x̂ + y·ŷ + z·ẑ and k̂ = k/|k| is the direction of propagation.
(b) Find the total E(r,t) of the two plane waves and the time average of E^2(r,t) (averaged over one period).
(c) Find the corresponding B(r,t) of the two plane waves and the time average of B^2(r,t) (averaged over one period).
(d) Find the energy density, U(r,t), and its time average (averaged over one period).
(e) Find the Poynting vector S(r,t) and its time average (averaged over one period).

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi, I’m working on Problem Set 6 for vibrations and waves, and I’m pretty stuck on the organ pipe normal modes in 6.1. I think the closed end and open end give different boundary conditions, but I’m not fully sure how to turn that into the first 3 modes and frequencies.
exchanges.tutor: Good starting point — boundary conditions are exactly the key here. So let's build from what you know: at a closed end, what is the air physically able to do, and what does that mean for the displacement ψ?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is starting fresh on 6.1. I should use the Socratic method to help them build from what they already know about boundary conditions, rather than explaining the conditions to them. A single focused question will get them thinking in the right direction without over-scaffolding.

exchanges.student: I think at the closed end the air can’t move back and forth, so the longitudinal displacement should be zero there. So maybe \( \psi(0,t)=0 \)?
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right. Now what about the open end at x = L? What can the air do there, and what does that imply for ψ?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the boundary condition at the closed end. I should affirm this briefly and immediately push them to think about the open end, which is the other half of what they need before they can draw the modes.

exchanges.student: This is the part I mix up. I think at the open end the air can move the most, so displacement is an antinode there, which means \( \psi \) is maximum at \( x=L \), right?
exchanges.tutor: Correct — the open end is a displacement antinode, meaning the displacement is free to be maximum there. In terms of a boundary condition, that translates to ∂ψ/∂x = 0 at x = L. Now, with a node at x = 0 and an antinode at x = L, what's the simplest wave pattern (first normal mode) that fits in the pipe?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified that the open end is a displacement antinode. I should confirm this and translate it into a mathematical boundary condition, then push them toward sketching the modes.

exchanges.student: I think that means a quarter of a wavelength fits in the pipe, so \(L=\lambda/4\) for the fundamental. Then \( \lambda_1=4L \).
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right. Now can you extend that pattern to the 2nd and 3rd normal modes? What fractions of a wavelength fit in the pipe for those?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the fundamental mode. I should confirm and push them to extend the pattern to the 2nd and 3rd modes, and then to compute the frequencies.

exchanges.student: I think only odd quarter-wavelength patterns work, but I’m not fully confident. So maybe \(L=3\lambda/4\) for the next one and \(L=5\lambda/4\) for the third, giving \(\lambda_2=4L/3\) and \(\lambda_3=4L/5\)?
exchanges.tutor: That's right — only odd harmonics fit, and you've got the wavelengths correctly. Now, how do you get the frequencies from those wavelengths? Think about what A tells you in the wave equation.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the pattern for all three modes. I should confirm and now push them to compute the actual frequencies using the wave equation constant A, which they haven't used yet.

exchanges.student: I think the wave speed is \(v=\sqrt{A}\), so here \(v=\sqrt{90000}=300\ \text{m/s}\). Then I’d use \(f=v/\lambda\), so I think the first three are \(300/(4L)\), \(300/(4L/3)\), and \(300/(4L/5)\), but I haven’t simplified them yet.
exchanges.tutor: Good — v = 300 m/s is correct, and your frequency expressions are right. Now just plug in L = 1.5 m and compute the three numerical frequencies. What do you get?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the wave speed and the frequency formula. I should encourage them to plug in L=1.5m and compute the actual numerical values, completing part (a).

exchanges.student: I get \(f_1 = 300/6 = 50\) Hz, \(f_2 = 300/2 = 150\) Hz, and \(f_3 = 300/(1.2) = 250\) Hz. So I think the first three frequencies are 50, 150, and 250 Hz.
exchanges.tutor: All three frequencies are correct. Now for part (b), you need to sketch the pressure rather than displacement. How does pressure relate to displacement — where are the pressure nodes and antinodes compared to the displacement nodes and antinodes?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly computed all three frequencies. I should confirm and move them along to part (b), which asks about pressure rather than displacement - a conceptually distinct step that requires understanding the relationship between displacement and pressure nodes/antinodes.

exchanges.student: I think they’re kind of opposite? Like where displacement has a node, pressure has an antinode, and where displacement has an antinode, pressure has a node. Is that the right way to think about the sketches?
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right. So applying that to this pipe: what does that make the pressure at x = 0 and at x = L for each mode?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the inverse relationship between pressure and displacement nodes/antinodes. I should confirm and push them to apply this to the specific boundary conditions of this pipe to describe what the pressure sketches look like.

exchanges.student: Then pressure should be an antinode at the closed end \(x=0\) and a node at the open end \(x=L\) for every mode. I’m guessing the pressure shapes are the same spatial wavelengths as before, just shifted so the maxima/minima happen where the displacement had nodes.
exchanges.tutor: That's exactly right — you've got part (b). Now for part (c): the pipe is changed to be open at both ends. What boundary condition does that impose at both x = 0 and x = L, and how does that change the wavelength of the fundamental?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly described the pressure boundary conditions and the relationship to the displacement sketches. They've now completed the conceptual work for part (b). I should confirm and move them to part (c), which is a relatively short calculation but requires a new setup.

exchanges.student: I think open at both ends means displacement antinodes at both ends, so the fundamental has half a wavelength in the pipe: \(L'=\lambda/2\). I’m not sure if I should use the old fundamental wavelength from part (a) directly here or compare frequencies another way.
exchanges.tutor: Good. The wave speed hasn't changed, so two waves have the same frequency if and only if they have the same wavelength. You already know λ₁ for the closed-open pipe — how does that help you find L'?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the boundary conditions and fundamental mode for an open-open pipe. They just need to connect the frequency-matching condition: same frequency means same wavelength (same wave speed), so they can directly equate the two fundamental wavelengths to find L'. I should nudge them to make that connection without giving it away.

grade.overview: The tutor conducted a clean Socratic dialogue throughout all 10 turns, consistently prompting the student to derive each step rather than providing answers directly. Boundary conditions, wavelengths, frequencies, pressure relationships, and the open-open pipe scenario were all handled through targeted questions that built on the student's prior correct responses. No direct answers, rewrites, or off-topic drift were observed.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never gave a final answer or near-submission-ready output; every turn ended with a question or prompt that required the student to do the reasoning. Key ideas (boundary conditions, wavelength fractions, frequency calculation, pressure-displacement relationship) were all student-generated. 1.2: The tutor consistently acknowledged correct student steps before advancing, and responses were well-calibrated to the student's demonstrated understanding. 1.3: The tutor provided implicit process feedback by confirming correct reasoning approaches (e.g., noting that only odd harmonics fit, that wave speed determines frequency). 2.1 and 2.2: No repetition without progress and no off-topic drift occurred; the conversation advanced steadily through part (a), (b), and into (c). 3.1 and 3.2: Responses were concise, focused, and supportive throughout, with no monologues or tone issues.
